name: data-slicer

metadata.name: data-slicer
metadata.summary: Tools for quick visualization of three dimensional datasets.

documentation:
![](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kuadrat/data_slicer/master/img/02_Data_Slicer_Logo.png)

# Data slicer 
The `data-slicer` package offers fast tools for inspection, visualization, 
slicing and analysis of 3(+) dimensional datasets at a general level.
It also provides a framework and building blocks for users to easily create 
more specialized and customized tools for their individual use cases.

![](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kuadrat/data_slicer/master/img/pit_demo.gif)

`data-slicer` was originally developed to deal with the high data throughput of 
modern measurement instruments, where quick visualizations and preliminary 
analyses are necessary to guide the direction of a measurement session.
However, the package is designed to be agnostic of the concrete use-case and 
all scientific, engineering, medical, artistic or other data driven 
disciplines where inspection and slicing of (hyper)cubes is required could 
potentially benefit from `data-slicer`.

## Documentation

This README just gives a minimal overview.
For more information, guides, examples and more, visit the documentation which is hosted 
by the friendly people over at ReadTheDocs.org:
https://data-slicer.readthedocs.io/en/latest/

## Installation

`data-slicer` should run on all platforms that support python and has been 
shown to run on Windows, macOS ans Linux.

The package can be installed from [PyPI](https://pypi.org/project/data-slicer/) 
using `pip install data_slicer`.
It is recommended to do this from within some sort of virtual environment.
Visit the documentation for more detailed instructions:
https://data-slicer.readthedocs.io/en/latest/installation.html

### Dependencies

This software is built upon on a number of other open-source frameworks.
The complete list of packages can be found in the file `requirements.txt`.
Most notably, this includes [matplotlib](https://matplotlib.org/), 
[numpy](https://numpy.org/) and 
[pyqtgraph](https://pyqtgraph.readthedocs.io/en/latest/).

## Citing

If you use `data-slicer` in your work, please credit it by citing the following publication:

[![DOI](https://joss.theoj.org/papers/10.21105/joss.02969/status.svg)](https://doi.org/10.21105/joss.02969)

Kramer et al., (2021). Visualization of Multi-Dimensional Data -- The data-slicer Package. Journal of Open Source Software, 6(60), 2969, https://doi.org/10.21105/joss.02969

## Contributing

You are welcome to help making this software package more useful!
You can do this by giving feedback, reporting bugs, issuing feature requests 
or fixing bugs and adding new features yourself. 
Furthermore, you can create and share your own plugins (refer to the 
[documentation](https://data-slicer.readthedocs.io/en/latest/)).

### Feedback, bugs and feature requests

The most straightforward and organized way to help improve this software is 
by opening an **issue** on [the github 
repository](https://github.com/kuadrat/data-slicer/issues).
To do this, navigate to the **Issues** tab and click **New issue**.
Please try to describe the bug you encountered or the new feature you would 
like to see as detailed as possible.
If you have several bugs/ideas please open a separate issue for each of them 
in order to keep the discussions focused.

If you have anything to tell me that does not seem to warrant opening an 
issue or you simply prefer to contact me directly you can do this via e-mail:
kevin.pasqual.kramer@protonmail.ch

### Contributing code

If you have fixed a bug or created a new feature in the source code yourself, 
it can be merged into this project.
Code contributions will be acknowledged in this README or, if the number of 
contributors grows too large, in a separate file.
If you are familiar with the workflow on github, please go ahead and create a 
pull request.
If you are unsure you can always contact me via e-mail (see above).

### Plugins

If you have created a PIT plugin, feel free to add it to the list below, 
either via pull request or through an e-mail (see above).
Also check the 
[documentation](https://data-slicer.readthedocs.io/en/latest/contributing.html) 
for a guide on how to create a plugin.

| Plugin Name (link) | Description | 
| ------------------ | ----------- |
| [ds-example-plugin](https://github.com/kuadrat/ds-example-plugin) | exists as a minimal example that can be used for guidance when creating your own plugins. A step-by-step tutorial on how it was made can be found in [the documentation](https://data-slicer.readthedocs.io/en/latest/contributing.html) |
| [ds-arpes-plugin](https://github.com/kuadrat/ds_arpes_plugin) | tools for angle-resolved photoelectron spectroscopy (ARPES) |

modules.name: data_slicer.set_up_logging
documentation:
This file is intended to be imported by scripts that want to output logging 
info of all the components they use.

modules.name: data_slicer.dataloading
documentation: Tools for putting different data formats into suitable numpy arrays.

functions.name: data_slicer.dataloading.load_data
documentation:
Try to load some dataset 'filename' by iterating through `all_dls` 
and appliyng the respective dataloader's load_data method. If it works: 
great. If not, try with the next dataloader. 
Collects and prints all raised exceptions in case that no dataloader 
succeeded.
Function to try all dataloaders in all_dls

functions.name: data_slicer.dataloading.three_d_to_txt
documentation:
Create a txt file that can be read by
{class}`~data_slicer.dataloading.Dataloader_txt`.

**Parameters**

```




outfilename

str; filename and/or path to the file to write into.

data

np.array; 3d array of the data.

axes

list; [xaxis, yaxis, zaxis]. Any of these can be None, in
which case incremental integers will be used.

force

boolean; Whether or not to overwrite an existing file. If
False and a file of outfilename exists, an Exception is
raised.
```
Convenience for creating txt files

functions.name: data_slicer.dataloading.dump
documentation:
Wrapper for {func}`pickle.dump`. Does not overwrite if a file of
the given name already exists, unless *force* is True.

**Parameters**

```




D

python object to be stored.

filename

str; name of the output file to create.

force

boolean; if True, overwrite existing files.
```
Function to create a python pickle file from a data namespace

classes.name: data_slicer.dataloading.Dataloader

methods.name: data_slicer.dataloading.Dataloader.print_m
documentation: Print message to console, adding the dataloader name.

methods.name: data_slicer.dataloading.Dataloader.load_data
documentation:
Method stub to be overwritten by subclasses. Return data in the 
form of an argparse.Namespace object *D* with the following structure: 

======  ============================================================
D.data  np.array containing the data.
D.axes  list of length len(D.data.shape). Contains a 1d np.array 
        representing each axis (or *None*)
======  ============================================================

Base dataloader class (interface) from which others inherit some
methods (specifically the `__repr__()` function).

classes.name: data_slicer.dataloading.Dataloader_Pickle

methods.name: data_slicer.dataloading.Dataloader_Pickle.load_data
documentation:
Load data that has been saved using python's `pickle` module. 
The data is assumed to be either just a naked array, a dictionary 
containing the keys *data* and *axes* or an argparse.Namespace 
instance, containing these same keys.

Confer documentation of
{func}`~data_slicer.dataloading.Dataloader_Pickle.load_data()`.

classes.name: data_slicer.dataloading.Dataloader_3dtxt

methods.name: data_slicer.dataloading.Dataloader_3dtxt.load_data
documentation:
Load data of shape (nx, ny, nz) that is stored in a .txt file in
the format:

```
#Z      Y       X       I(X, Y, Z)
z0      y0      x0      I(0, 0, 0)
z1      y0      x0      I(0, 0, 1)
z2      y0      x0      I(0, 0, 2)
z3      y0      x0      I(0, 0, 3)
...
z(nz-1) y0      x0      I(0, 0, nz-1)
z(nz)   y0      x0      I(0, 0, nz)
z0      y1      x0      I(0, 1, 0)
z1      y1      x0      I(0, 1, 1)
z2      y1      x0      I(0, 1, 2)
...
z(nz-1) y1      x0      I(0, 1, nz-1)
z(nz)   y1      x0      I(0, 1, nz)
...
...
z0      y(ny)   x0      I(0, ny, 0)
z1      y(ny)   x0      I(0, ny, 1)
...
z(nz)   y(ny)   x0      I(0, ny, nz)
z0      y0      x1      I(1, 0, 0)
z1      y0      x1      I(1, 0, 1)
...
...
z(nz)   y(ny)   x(nx)   I(nz, ny, nx)
```

Confer documentation of
{func}`~data_slicer.dataloading.Dataloader_3dtxt.load_data()`.

classes.name: data_slicer.cutline.CustomizableLineSegmentROI

methods.name: data_slicer.cutline.CustomizableLineSegmentROI.set_hover_pen
documentation:
Set the pen used for drawing this widget when it is in the 
`hovered` state. Accepts function arguments to :func: `mkPen 
pyqtgraph.mkPen`

methods.name: data_slicer.cutline.CustomizableLineSegmentROI._makePen
documentation:
Overrided parent's :func: `_makePen 
<pyqtgrapch.graphicsItems.ROI.makePen()>` function to allow custom 
hover styles.

Subclass of {class}`LineSegmentROI
<pyqtgraph.LineSegmentROI>`. Implements a few features that were missing
from the parent class, namely customizable hover pen.

classes.name: data_slicer.cutline.CustomizableHandle

methods.name: data_slicer.cutline.CustomizableHandle.set_hover_pen
documentation:
Set the pen used for drawing this widget when it is in the
*hovered* state. Accepts function arguments to :func:\`
pyqtgraph.mkPen\`

methods.name: data_slicer.cutline.CustomizableHandle.hoverEvent
documentation:
This just copies the code of the parent class with the difference 
of a variable hover pen.

Same concept as {class}`CustomizableLineSegmentROI
<data_slicer.cutline.CustomizableLineSegmentROI`>. Subclass to allow
customization of hover pen.

:::{Note}
This class is unused because that would require setting these handles
as the handles for `CustomizableLineSegmentROI`. Too much work for a
relatively unimportant feature.
:::

classes.name: data_slicer.cutline.Cutline

methods.name: data_slicer.cutline.Cutline.add_to_plot
documentation:
Add this cutline to a {class}`PlotWidget <pyqtgraph.PlotWidget>`.
This is effectively implemented by setting this {class}`Cutline
<data_slicer.cutline.Cutline>`'s plot attribute to the given *plot_widget*.

methods.name: data_slicer.cutline.Cutline.initialize
documentation: Emits {signal}`sig_initialized`.
# Signal connection: whenever the viewRange changes, the cutline should
# be updated. Make sure to not accumulate connections by trying to
# disconnect first.
try :
self.plot.sigRangeChanged.disconnect(self.initialize)
except TypeError :
pass
self.plot.sig_axes_changed.connect(self.initialize)
self.plot.sigRangeChanged.connect(self.initialize)

methods.name: data_slicer.cutline.Cutline.set_handle_style
documentation: Set the size and pen of the handles.

methods.name: data_slicer.cutline.Cutline.recenter
documentation: Put the ROI in the center of the current plot.

methods.name: data_slicer.cutline.Cutline.calculate_endpoints
documentation:
Get sensible initial values for the endpoints of the
{class}`LineSegmentROI <pyqtgraph.LineSegmentROI>` from the
{class}`pyqtrgaph.PlotWidget`'s current view range. Depending on the
state of `self.orientation` these endpoints correspond either to a
vertical or horizontal line centered at the center of the plot and
spanning exactly the whole plot range.

Returns a tuple of len(2) lists: (lower_left, top_right)
corresponding to the two endpoints.

methods.name: data_slicer.cutline.Cutline.flip_orientation
documentation:
Change the cutline's orientation from vertical to horitontal or 
vice-versa and re-initialize it in the new orientation.

methods.name: data_slicer.cutline.Cutline.get_array_region
documentation:
Wrapper for the underlying ROI's
{meth}`~data_slicer.cutline.Cutline.roi.getArrayRegion`.

Wrapper class allowing easy adding and removing of
{class}`pyqtgraph.LineSegmentROI` to a
{class}`pyqtgraph.PlotWidget`.
It both
has-a LineSegmentROI
and
has-a PlotWidget
and handles interactions between the two.

Needs to inherit from {class}`pyqtgraph.qt.QtCore.QObject` in order to
have signals.

**Signals**

```




sig_region_changed

wraps the underlying 's sigRegionChange.
Emitted whenever the ROI is moved or changed.

sig_initialized

emitted when a new  has been
created and assigned as this 's .
```

classes.name: data_slicer.model.ModelError
documentation:
Base class for {class}`Model <data_slicer.model.Model>` related
errors.

classes.name: data_slicer.model.UndefinedModelError
documentation:
Error raised when an operation would require the *model* attribute of 
:class: `Model <data_slicer.model.Model>` but it is not found.

classes.name: data_slicer.model.UndefinedAxisError
documentation:
Error raised when an operation would require all the axes in the 
*axes* attribute of :class: `Model <data_slicer.model.Model>` but at least
one of them is not found.

classes.name: data_slicer.model.Model

methods.name: data_slicer.model.Model.__init__
documentation:
**Parameters**

```




model

callable; a python function representing the model.
```

:::{seealso}
{meth}`set_model <data_slicer.model.Model.set_model>`
:::

methods.name: data_slicer.model.Model.set_model
documentation:
Specify the function that represents the model. This should be a
function with the call signature:

```
model(axis1, axis2, ..., axisN, kwarg1=kwarg1_default, ...,
      kwargN=kwargN_default, **kwargs)
```

I.e. all the positional arguments (*axis1* to *axisN*) correspond to
the required input variables. The keyword arguments (*kwarg1* to
*kwargN*) as well as further unspecified *kwargs* can be used for the
model parameters.

Information about the number of arguments is obtained through
introspection.

**Parameters**

```




model

callable; a python function representing the model.
```

methods.name: data_slicer.model.Model._check_if_model_defined
documentation: Raise an appropriate error if no model function is found.

methods.name: data_slicer.model.Model._check_if_axes_defined
documentation:
Raise an appropriate error if at least one of the necessary axes 
has not been properliy defined.

methods.name: data_slicer.model.Model.set_axes
documentation:
Set all axes (inputs for the model). The axes supplied here can
either be 1 dimensional array-like objects representing the actual
values at which the model should be evaluated or simply the start and
stop values for the range in which the model will be evaluated.
If the length of any axis is smaller than Model.MIN_AXIS_LENGTH or if
just start and stop values are given, linearly space values between
start and stop will be given.

**Parameters**

```




axes

list of len(self.n_args); an error will be thrown if the number
of supplied axes does not match what is necessary for self.model.
```

:::{seealso}
{meth}`set_axis <data_slicer.model.Model.set_axis>` to set just
one specific axis.
:::

methods.name: data_slicer.model.Model.set_axis
documentation:
Set the axis (input to the model) at position *dim*. See
documentation of {meth}`set_axes <data_slicer.model.Model.set_axes>`
for more details.

**Parameters**

```




axis

1d array-like; the values at which the model should be
evaluated along this dimension. If the length is smaller than
Model.MIN_AXIS_LENGTH, linearly spaced values between the first
and last value in axis will be created.
```

:::{seealso}
{meth}`set_axes <data_slicer.model.Model.set_axes>` to set all
axes at once.
:::

methods.name: data_slicer.model.Model.get_axes_dims
documentation: Return the length of all axes that are defined.

methods.name: data_slicer.model.Model.calculate_model_data
documentation:
Evaluate the given model function {meth}`model
<data_slicer.model.Model.model>` at every point in the hypervolume
defined by the *axes*. This means that every possible combination of
coordinates of all *axes* is created, and the model evaluated at
every such point.

**Parameters**

```




axes

if specified, this is passed on to . Otherwise the previously
set axes will be used.

kwargs

all keyword arguments are passed to the model function.
```

methods.name: data_slicer.model.Model.make_slice
documentation:
Return a slice out of the model data. If the data has not yet
been calculated, try to do it first.
This wraps {func}`make_slice <data_slicer.utilities.make_slice>`.
Confer respective documentation for information on the arguments.

methods.name: data_slicer.model.Model.get_isocurve
documentation:
:::{warning}
Only possible for 2D models (i.e. self.n_args==2).
:::

Return an isocurve ({class}`IsocurveItem
<pyqtgraph.graphicsItems.IsocurveItem>` of the model data at the
selected *level*.

This uses pyqtgraph's icocurve function, which is based on the
marching squares algorithm.

**Parameters**

```




level

float; value at which the isocurve is generated.

pen

arguments for the visual properties of the isocurve. Can be
anything which is valid for .
```

**Returns**

```




isocurveItem

```

methods.name: data_slicer.model.Model.get_values_around

documentation:
General object that allows calculating a model over some input axes. It 
also provides functionalities to extract different slices from the 
calculated data.

modules.name: data_slicer.tests.test_pit
documentation: Simply start up PIT with its example data.

functions.name: data_slicer.tests.test_freeslice.test_freeslice
documentation: Test creating the freeslice window.

functions.name: data_slicer.tests.test_threedwidget.create_threedwindow
documentation:
Create an example window that makes use of {class}`ThreeDWidget
<data_slicer.widgets.ThreeDWidget>` to display a slice plane through a 3D
data cube.

For convenience with
{func}`~data_slicer.tests.test_threedwidget.test_threedwidget`, this
function returns the created QWindow, the ThreeDWidget as well as the two
QPushButtons.

functions.name: data_slicer.tests.test_threedwidget.test_threedwidget
documentation:
Create a ThreeDWidget and use the arrow keys to move the slice plane 
up and down.

modules.name: data_slicer.dsviewbox
documentation: Subclass of ViewBox with custom menu items.

classes.name: data_slicer.dsviewbox.DSViewBoxMenu
documentation: Subclass of ViewBoxMenu with custom menu items.

classes.name: data_slicer.dsviewbox.DSViewBox
documentation:
Subclass of ViewBox with custom menu items, as defined in
{class}`~data_slicer.dsviewbox.DSViewBoxMenu`.

modules.name: data_slicer.pit
documentation:
PIT is a widget that combines slices from different sides with intensity 
distribution curves and other convenient features.

functions.name: data_slicer.pit.start_main_window
documentation: Hook for setuptools entry point

classes.name: data_slicer.pit.EmbedIPython
documentation: Some voodoo to get an ipython console in a Qt application.

classes.name: data_slicer.pit.PITDataHandler

methods.name: data_slicer.pit.PITDataHandler.get_config_dir
documentation: Return the path to the configuration directory on this system.

methods.name: data_slicer.pit.PITDataHandler.get_data
documentation:
Convenience `getter` method. Allows writing `self.get_data()`
instead of `self.data.get_value()`.

methods.name: data_slicer.pit.PITDataHandler.set_data
documentation:
Convenience `setter` method. Allows writing `self.set_data(d)`
instead of `self.data.set_value(d)`.
Additionally allows setting new *axes*. If *axes* is `None`, the axes
are reset to pixels.

methods.name: data_slicer.pit.PITDataHandler.prepare_data
documentation:
Load the specified data and prepare the corresponding z range. 
Then display the newly loaded data.

**Parameters**

====  ==================================================================
data  3d array; the data to display
axes  len(3) list or array of 1d-arrays or None; the units along the 
      x, y and z axes respectively. If any of those is *None*, pixels 
      are used.
====  ==================================================================

methods.name: data_slicer.pit.PITDataHandler.load
documentation: Alias to {func}`open <data_slicer.pit.PITDataHandler.open>`.

methods.name: data_slicer.pit.PITDataHandler.open
documentation:
Open a file that's readable by {mod}`dataloading
<data_slicer.dataloading>`.

methods.name: data_slicer.pit.PITDataHandler.get_main_data
documentation: Return the 2d array that is currently displayed in the main plot.

methods.name: data_slicer.pit.PITDataHandler.get_cut_data
documentation: Return the 2d array that is currently displayed in the cut plot.

methods.name: data_slicer.pit.PITDataHandler.get_hprofile
documentation:
Return an array containing the y values displayed in the
horizontal profile plot (mw.y_plot).

:::{seealso}
{func}`data_slicer.imageplot.CursorPlot.get_data`
:::

methods.name: data_slicer.pit.PITDataHandler.get_vprofile
documentation:
Return an array containing the x values displayed in the
vertical profile plot (mw.x_plot).

:::{seealso}
{func}`data_slicer.imageplot.CursorPlot.get_data`
:::

methods.name: data_slicer.pit.PITDataHandler.get_iprofile
documentation:
Return an array containing the y values displayed in the
integrated intensity profile plot (mw.integrated_plot).

:::{seealso}
{func}`data_slicer.imageplot.CursorPlot.get_data`
:::

methods.name: data_slicer.pit.PITDataHandler.update_z_range
documentation:
When new data is loaded or the axes are rolled, the limits and 
allowed values along the z dimension change.

methods.name: data_slicer.pit.PITDataHandler.reset_data
documentation:
Put all data and metadata into its original state, as if it was 
just loaded from file.
self.z.set_value(self.zmin)

methods.name: data_slicer.pit.PITDataHandler.prepare_axes
documentation:
Create a list containing the three original x-, y- and z-axes 
and replace *None* with the amount of pixels along the given axis.

methods.name: data_slicer.pit.PITDataHandler.on_data_change
documentation: Update self.main_window.image_data and replot.

methods.name: data_slicer.pit.PITDataHandler.on_z_dim_change
documentation:
Called when either completely new data is loaded or the dimension
from which we look at the data changed (e.g. through {func}`roll_axes
<data_slicer.pit.PITDataHandler.roll_axes>`).
Update the z range and the integrated intensity plot.

methods.name: data_slicer.pit.PITDataHandler.update_image_data
documentation:
Get the right (possibly integrated) slice out of *self.data*, 
apply postprocessings and store it in *self.image_data*. 
Skip this if the z value happens to be out of range, which can happen 
if the image data changes and the z scale hasn't been updated yet.

methods.name: data_slicer.pit.PITDataHandler.roll_axes
documentation:
Change the way we look at the data cube. While initially we see 
an Y vs. X slice in the main plot, roll it to Z vs. Y. A second call 
would roll it to X vs. Z and, finally, a third call brings us back to 
the original situation.

**Parameters**

=  =====================================================================
i  int; Number of dimensions to roll.
=  =====================================================================

methods.name: data_slicer.pit.PITDataHandler._roll_axes
documentation:
Backend for {func}`roll_axes <arpys.pit.PITDataHandler.roll_axes>`
that allows suppressing updating the roll-state, which is useful for
{func}`reset_data <arpys.pit.PITDataHandler.reset_data>`.

methods.name: data_slicer.pit.PITDataHandler.lineplot
documentation:
Create a matplotlib figure with *n* lines extracted out of one of the
visible plots. The lines are normalized to their global maximum and
shifted from each other by *offset*.
See {func}`get_lines <data_slicer.utilities.get_lines>` for more
options on the extraction of the lines.
This wraps the {class}`ImagePlot <data_slicer.imageplot.ImagePlot>`'s
lineplot method.

**Parameters**

```




plot

str; either "main" or "cut", specifies from which
plot to extract the lines.

dim

int; either 0 or 1, specifies in which direction to
take the lines.

ax

matplotlib.axes.Axes; the axes in which to plot. If
None, create a new figure with a fresh axes.

n

int; number of lines to extract.

offset

float; spacing between neighboring lines.

lw

float; linewidth of the plotted lines.

color

any color argument understood by matplotlib; color
of the plotted lines.

label_fmt

str; a format string for the ticklabels.

n_ticks

int; number of ticks to print.

getlines_kwargs

other kwargs are passed to 
```

**Returns**

```




lines2ds

list of Line2D objects; the drawn lines.

xticks

list of float; locations of the 0 intensity value of
each line.

xtickvalues

list of float; if momenta were supplied, corresponding
xtick values in units of momenta. Otherwise this is
just a copy of xticks.

xticklabels

list of str; xtickvalues formatted according to
label_fmt.
```

:::{seealso}
{func}`get_lines <data_slicer.utilities.get_lines>`
:::

methods.name: data_slicer.pit.PITDataHandler.plot_all_slices
documentation:
Wrapper for {func}`plot_cuts <data_slicer.utilities.plot_cuts>`.
Plot all (or only the ones specified by `zs`) slices along dimension
`dim` on separate suplots onto matplotlib figures.

**Parameters**

```




dim

int; one of (0,1,2). Dimension along which to take the cuts.

integrate

int or 'full'; number of slices to integrate around each
extracted cut. If 'full', take the maximum number possible,
depending on zs and whether the number of cuts is reduced
due to otherwise exceeding max_nfigs.

zs

1D np.array; selection of indices along dimension .
Only the given indices will be plotted.

labels

1D array/list of length z. Optional labels to assign to the
different cuts. By default the values of the respective axis
are used. Set to None to suppress labels.

max_ppf

int; maximum number of plots per figure.

max_nfigs

int; maximum number of figures that are created. If more
would be necessary to display all plots, a warning is
issued and only every N'th plot is created, where N is
chosen such that the whole 'range' of plots is represented
on the figures.

kwargs

dict; keyword arguments passed on to .
Additionally, the kwarg  for power-law color mapping
is accepted.
```

:::{seealso}
{func}`~data_slicer.utilities.plot_cuts`
:::

methods.name: data_slicer.pit.PITDataHandler.overlay_model
documentation:
Display a model over the data. *model* should be function of two
variables, namely the currently displayed x- and y-axes.

**Parameters**

```




model

callable or ;
```

:::{seealso}
{class}`Model <data_slicer.model.Model>`
:::

methods.name: data_slicer.pit.PITDataHandler.remove_model
documentation: Remove the current model's visible and invisible parts.

Object that keeps track of a set of 3D data and allows
manipulations on it. In a Model-View-Controller framework this could be
seen as the Model, while {class}`MainWindow <data_slicer.pit.MainWindow>`
would be the View part.

classes.name: data_slicer.pit.MainWindow

methods.name: data_slicer.pit.MainWindow._init_UI
documentation: Initialize the elements of the user interface.

methods.name: data_slicer.pit.MainWindow._align
documentation:
Align all the GUI elements in the QLayout:

```
0   1   2   3   4
```

(Units of subdivision [sd])

methods.name: data_slicer.pit.MainWindow._autoload_plugins
documentation:
Load all the plugins specified in the config file
`CONF_DIR/plugins/autoload.txt`.

methods.name: data_slicer.pit.MainWindow.load_plugin
documentation:
Load a user supplied plugin and connect the plugin's `main` class 
with PIT.
The plugin should be a python module which is placed in the 
pythonpath or in the `plugins` directory. 

**Parameters**

===========  ===========================================================
plugin_name  str; name of the plugin module as it appears in the 
             `plugins` directory.
===========  ===========================================================

methods.name: data_slicer.pit.MainWindow.print_to_console
documentation: Print a *message* to the embedded ipython console.

methods.name: data_slicer.pit.MainWindow.brain
documentation:
Load the example dataset from an MRI brain scan. 

This example data is taken from the OpenNeuro database.
Openneuro Accession Number: ds000108
Authored by: Wager, T.D., Davidson, M.L., Hughes, B.L., Lindquist, 
M.A., Ochsner, K.N. (2008). Prefrontal-subcortical pathways mediating 
successful emotion regulation. Neuron, 59(6):1037-50. 
doi: ``10.1016/j.neuron.2008.09.006``

methods.name: data_slicer.pit.MainWindow.update_main_plot
documentation:
Change *self.main_plot\*\`s currently displayed
\:class:\`image_item \<data_slicer.imageplot.ImagePlot.image_item>\` to
the slice of \*self.data_handler.data* corresponding to the current
value of *self.z*.

methods.name: data_slicer.pit.MainWindow.set_axes
documentation:
Set the x- and y-scales of the plots. The {class}`ImagePlot
<data_slicer.imageplot.ImagePlot>` object takes care of keeping the
scales as they are, once they are set.

methods.name: data_slicer.pit.MainWindow.update_xy_plots
documentation: Update the x and y profile plots.

methods.name: data_slicer.pit.MainWindow.set_cmap
documentation:
Set the colormap to *cmap* where *cmap* is one of the names
registered in {mod}`<data_slicer.cmaps>` which includes all
matplotlib and custom cmaps.

methods.name: data_slicer.pit.MainWindow.cmap_changed
documentation:
Recalculate the lookup table and redraw the plots such that the 
changes are immediately reflected.

methods.name: data_slicer.pit.MainWindow.transpose
documentation:
Transpose the main_plot, i.e. swap out its x- and y-axes. This
wraps the main_plot's {meth}`transpose
<data_slicer.imageplot.ImagePlot.transpose>` method.

methods.name: data_slicer.pit.MainWindow.rotate
documentation: Rotate the main image by the given angle *alpha* (in degrees).

methods.name: data_slicer.pit.MainWindow.redraw_plots
documentation: Redraw plotted data to reflect changes in data or its colors.

methods.name: data_slicer.pit.MainWindow.set_image
documentation:
Wraps the underlying ImagePlot3d's set_image method.
See {func}`~data_slicer.imageplot.ImagePlot3d.set_image`. *image* can
be *None* i.e. in order to just update the plot with a new colormap.

methods.name: data_slicer.pit.MainWindow.update_cut
documentation:
Take a cut of *self.data_handler.data* along *self.cutline*. This 
is used to update only the cut plot without affecting the main plot.

methods.name: data_slicer.pit.MainWindow.on_cutline_initialized
documentation:
Need to reconnect the signal to the cut_plot. And directly update 
the cut_plot.

methods.name: data_slicer.pit.MainWindow.on_gamma_slider_move
documentation: When the user moves the gamma slider, update gamma.

methods.name: data_slicer.pit.MainWindow.on_vmax_slider_move
documentation: When the user moves the vmax slider, update vmax.

methods.name: data_slicer.pit.MainWindow.set_alpha
documentation:
Set the alpha value of the currently used cmap. *alpha* can be a
single float or an array of length `len(self.cmap.color)`.

methods.name: data_slicer.pit.MainWindow.set_gamma
documentation:
Set the exponent for the power-law norm that maps the colors to
values. I.e. the values where the colours are defined are mapped like
`y=x**gamma`.

methods.name: data_slicer.pit.MainWindow.set_vmax
documentation:
Set the relative maximum of the colormap. I.e. the colors are 
mapped to the range `min(data)` - `vmax*max(data)`.

methods.name: data_slicer.pit.MainWindow.cmap_changed
documentation:
Recalculate the lookup table and redraw the plots such that the 
changes are immediately reflected.

The main window of PIT. Defines the basic GUI layouts and 
acts as the controller, keeping track of the data and handling the 
communication between the different GUI elements.

modules.name: data_slicer.cmaps
documentation:
Convert some of the nicer matplotlib and kustom colormaps to pyqtgraph 
colormaps.

functions.name: data_slicer.cmaps.convert_matplotlib_to_pyqtgraph
documentation:
Take a matplotlib colormap and convert it to a pyqtgraph ColorMap.

**Parameters**

```




matplotlib_cmap

either a str representing the name of a matplotlib
colormap or a
 or
 instance.

alpha

float or array of same length as there are defined
colors in the matplotlib cmap; the alpha (transparency)
value to be assigned to the whole cmap. matplotlib cmaps
default to 1.
```

**Returns**

```




pyqtgraph_cmap

```

functions.name: data_slicer.cmaps.convert_ds_to_matplotlib
documentation:
Create a matplotlib colormap from a {class}`ds_cmap
<data_slicer.cmaps.ds_cmap>` instance.

**Parameters**

```




data_slicer_cmap



cmap_name

str; optional name for the created cmap.
```

**Returns**

```




matplotlib_cmap

```

functions.name: data_slicer.cmaps.load_custom_cmap
documentation:
Create a {class}`ds_cmap <data_slicer.cmaps.ds_cmap>` instance from
data stored in a file with three columns, red, green and blue - either in
integer form from 0-255 or as floats from 0.0 to 1.0 (ignores fourth
alpha column).
The suffix can be left out in the file name.
Also, an `_r` can be appended to the colormap name to indicate that the
inverse of a cmap is requested (e.g. my_cmap_r will give the reverse of
my_cmap).

functions.name: data_slicer.cmaps.load_cmap
documentation:
Return a ds_cmap object for the colormap specified by *cmap_name*.
*cmap_name* can be either
    
    1. one of the matplotlib colormap names

    2. one of the colormap names supplied by the data slicer package. At 
    the time of this writing, these are `rainbow_light`, `neutrons` and 
    `kocean`. This list is not regularly updated. Check the .cmap files 
    in the data/ directory of the installation (loacation depends on 
    your system) to find out all options.

    3. any file found in the cmaps/ subdirectory of your config directory 
    (check documentation).

functions.name: data_slicer.cmaps.load_user_cmaps
documentation: Append user supplied colormaps to the dictionary *cmaps*.
Add user supplied colormaps

classes.name: data_slicer.cmaps.ds_cmap
documentation:
Simple subclass of {class}`pyqtgraph.ColorMap`. Adds vmax,
powerlaw normalization and a convenience function to change alpha.

:::{versionchanged} 1.0.1 Made this an empty shell for fixing a bug with a change in colormaps in :module:`pyqtgraph`.
:::

classes.name: data_slicer.cmaps.ds_cmap_legacy

methods.name: data_slicer.cmaps.ds_cmap_legacy.apply_transformations
documentation:
Recalculate the positions where the colormapping is defined by
applying (in sequence) alpha, then a linear map to the range
[0, vmax] and finally the powerlaw scaling: `pos' = pos**gamma`.

methods.name: data_slicer.cmaps.ds_cmap_legacy.set_gamma
documentation:
Set the exponent for the power-law norm that maps the colors to
values. I.e. the values where the colours are defined are mapped like
`y=x**gamma`.

methods.name: data_slicer.cmaps.ds_cmap_legacy.set_alpha
documentation:
Set the value of alpha for the whole colormap to *alpha* where
*alpha* can be a float or an array of length `len(self.color)`.

methods.name: data_slicer.cmaps.ds_cmap_legacy.set_vmax
documentation:
Set the relative (to the maximum of the data) maximum of the 
colorscale.

Simple subclass of {class}`pyqtgraph.ColorMap`. Adds vmax,
powerlaw normalization and a convenience function to change alpha.

:::{versionchanged} 1.0.1 Renamed this to `ds_cmap_legacy` in order to fix a bug due to a change in colormaps in :module:`pyqtgraph`.
:::

functions.name: data_slicer.utilities.indexof
documentation:
Return the first index of the value in the array closest to the given
`value`.

Example:

```
>>> a = np.array([1, 0, 0, 2, 1])
>>> indexof(0, a)
1
>>> indexof(0.9, a)
0
```

functions.name: data_slicer.utilities.pop_kwarg
documentation:
Check if a keyword *name* appears in the dictionary *kwargs*. If yes, 
remove it from the dictionary, returning its value. If no, return the 
value specified by *default*.

functions.name: data_slicer.utilities.make_slice_3d
documentation:
---
deprecated: true
---

:::{warning}
Use {func}`make_slice <data_slicer.utilities.make_slice>`
instead. (though this ~might~ be slightly faster for 3d datasets)
:::

Create a slice out of the 3d data (l x m x n) along dimension d
(0,1,2) at index i. Optionally integrate around i.

**Parameters**

```




data

array-like; data of the shape (x, y, z)

d

int, d in (0, 1, 2); dimension along which to slice

i

int, 0 <= i < data.size[d]; The index at which to create the slice

integrate

int, 0 <= integrate < |i - n|; the number of slices above
and below slice i over which to integrate

silent

bool; toggle warning messages
```

**Returns**

```




res

np.array; Slice at index with dimensions shape[:d] + shape[d+1:]
where shape = (x, y, z).
```

functions.name: data_slicer.utilities.make_slice
documentation:
Take a slice out of an N dimensional dataset *data* at *index* along
dimension *dim*. Optionally integrate by +- *integrate* channels around
*index*.
If *data* has shape:

```
(n0, n1, ..., n(dim-1), n(dim), n(dim+1), ..., n(N-1))
```

the result will be of dimension N-1 and have shape:

```
(n0, n1, ..., n(dim-1), n(dim+1), ..., n(N-1))
```

or in other words:

```
shape(result) = shape(data)[:dim] + shape(data)[dim+1:]
```

.

**Parameters**

```




data

array-like; N dimensional dataset.

dim

int, 0 <= d < N; dimension along which to slice.

index

int, 0 <= index < data.size[d]; The index at which to create
the slice.

integrate

int, 0 <= integrate < |index|; the number of slices above
and below slice index over which to integrate. A warning is
issued if the integration range would exceed the data (can be
turned off with silent).

silent

bool; toggle warning messages.
```

**Returns**

```




res

np.array; slice at index alond dim with dimensions shape[:d] +
shape[d+1:].
```

functions.name: data_slicer.utilities.roll_array
documentation:
Cycle the arrangement of the dimensions in an *N* dimensional array.
For example, change an X-Y-Z arrangement to Y-Z-X.

**Parameters**

```




a

array of N dimensions, i.e. .

i

int; number of dimensions to roll
```

**Returns**

```




res

array of N dimensions where the axes have been rearranged as
follows:

before: `shape(a) = (d[0], d[1], ..., d[N])`
after:  `shape(res) = (d[(0+i)%N], d[(1+i)%N], ..., d[(N+i)%N])`
```

functions.name: data_slicer.utilities.get_lines
documentation:
Extract *n* evenly spaced rows/columns from data along dimension *dim*
between indices *i0* and *i1*. The extracted lines are normalized and offset
such that they can be nicely plotted close by each other - as is done, for
example in {func}`lineplot <data_slicer.pit.PITDataHandler.lineplot>`.

**Parameters**

```




data

2d np.array; the data from which to extract lines.

n

int; the number of lines to extract.

dim

int; either 0 or 1, specifying the dimension along which to
extract lines.

i0

int; starting index in data along dim.

i1

int; ending index in data along dim.

offset

float; how much to vertically translate each successive line.

integrate

int or other; specifies how many channels around each line
index should be integrated over. If anything but a small
enough integer is given, defaults to the maximally available
integration range.

kwargs

any other passed keyword arguments are discarded.
```

**Returns**

```




lines

list of 1d np.arrays; the extracted lines.

indices

list of int; the indices at which the lines were extracted.
```

functions.name: data_slicer.utilities.plot_cuts
documentation:
Plot all (or only the ones specified by `zs`) cuts along dimension `dim`
on separate subplots onto matplotlib figures.

**Parameters**

```




data

3D np.array with shape (z,y,x); the data cube.

dim

int; one of (0,1,2). Dimension along which to take the cuts.

integrate

int or 'full'; number of slices to integrate around each
extracted cut. If 'full', take the maximum number possible,
depending whether the number of cuts is reduced due to
otherwise exceeding max_nfigs. 'full' does not work if zs
are given.

zs

1D np.array; selection of indices along dimension . Only
the given indices will be plotted.

labels

1D array/list of length z. Optional labels to assign to the
different cuts

max_ppf

int; maximum number of plots per figure.

max_nfigs

int; maximum number of figures that are created. If more would
be necessary to display all plots, a warning is issued and
only every N'th plot is created, where N is chosen such that
the whole 'range' of plots is represented on the figures.

kwargs

dict; keyword arguments passed on to .
Additionally, the kwarg  for power-law color mapping
is accepted.
```

functions.name: data_slicer.utilities.get_contours
documentation:
Use matplotlib`s contour function to get contour lines where the 2 
dimensional dataset *data* intersects *levels*. 

**Parameters**

======  ====================================================================
data    2d-array; shape (nx, ny)
x       array-like; can be a linear array of shape (nx) or a meshgrid of 
        shape (nx, ny)
y       array-like; can be a linear array of shape (ny) or a meshgrid of 
        shape (nx, ny)
levels  float or list of float; the levels at which to extract the 
        contour lines. Due to a matplotlib limitation, these numbers have 
        to be in ascending order.
======  ====================================================================

**Returns**

========  ==================================================================
contours  list of 2d-arrays; each array of shape (2, N) contains the x and
          y coordinates of a contour line.
========  ==================================================================

classes.name: data_slicer.utilities.TracedVariable

methods.name: data_slicer.utilities.TracedVariable.set_value
documentation: Emit sig_value_changed and set the internal self._value.

methods.name: data_slicer.utilities.TracedVariable.get_value
documentation:
Emit sig_value_changed and return the internal self.\_value.

:::{warning}
the signal is emitted here before the caller actually receives
the return value. This could lead to unexpected behaviour.
:::

methods.name: data_slicer.utilities.TracedVariable.on_change
documentation:
Convenience wrapper for {class}`Signal
<pyqtgraph.Qt.QtCore.Signal>`'s 'connect'.

methods.name: data_slicer.utilities.TracedVariable.on_read
documentation:
Convenience wrapper for {class}`Signal
<pyqtgraph.Qt.QtCore.Signal>`'s 'connect'.

methods.name: data_slicer.utilities.TracedVariable.set_allowed_values
documentation:
Define a set/range/list of values that are allowed for this
Variable. Once set, all future calls to set_value will automatically
try to pick the most reasonable of the allowed values to assign.

Emits {signal}`sig_allowed_values_changed`

**Parameters**

```




values

iterable; The complete list of allowed (numerical) values. This
is converted to a sorted np.array internally. If values is
, all restrictions on allowed values will be lifted and
all values are allowed.
```

methods.name: data_slicer.utilities.TracedVariable.find_closest_allowed
documentation:
Return the value of the element in self.allowed_values (if set) 
that is closest to `value`.

A pyqt implementaion of tkinter's/Tcl's traced variables using Qt's
signaling mechanism.
Basically this is just a wrapper around any python object which ensures
that pyQt signals are emitted whenever the object is accessed or changed.

In order to use pyqt's signals, this has to be a subclass of
{class}`QObject <pyqtgraph.Qt.QtCore.QObject>`.

**Attributes**

```




_value

the python object represented by this
TracedVariable instance. Should never be
accessed directly but only through the getter
and setter methods.

sig_value_changed

;
the signal that is emitted whenever
self._value is changed.

sig_value_read

;
the signal that is emitted whenever
self._value is read.

sig_allowed_values_changed

;
the signal that is emitted whenever
self.allowed_values are set or unset.

allowed_values

; a sorted
list of all values that self._value can
assume. If set, all tries to set the value
will automatically set it to the closest
allowed one.
```

documentation:
Put all sub-widgets and elements into the layout as follows:

```
   0     1     2
+-----+-----+-----+
|                 | 0
+                 +
|                 | 1
+     GLView      +
|                 | 2
+                 +
|                 | 3
+-----+-----+-----+
| xy  | yz  | zx  | 4
+-----+-----+-----+

5 units in height
3 units in width
```

A {class}`ThreeDWidget <data_slicer.widgets.ThreeDWidget>` which
represents its xy plane in an additional 2D panel.
In this "selector" panel, there's a {class}`Cutline
<data_slicer.cutline.Cutline>` with which arbitrary slices can be

documentation: Add this crosshair to a Qt widget.

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.Crosshair.remove_from
documentation: Remove this crosshair from a pyqtgraph widget.

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.Crosshair.set_color
documentation:
Set the color and hover color of both InfiniteLines that make up 
the crosshair. The arguments can be any pyqtgraph compatible color 
specifiers.

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.Crosshair.set_movable
documentation: Set whether or not this crosshair can be dragged by the mouse.

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.Crosshair.update_position_h
documentation:
Callback for the {signal}`sig_value_changed
<data_slicer.utilities.TracedVariable.sig_value_changed>`. Whenever the
value of this TracedVariable is updated (possibly from outside this
Crosshair object), put the crosshair to the appropriate position.

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.Crosshair.update_position_v
documentation: Confer update_position_h.

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.Crosshair.on_dragged_h
documentation:
Callback for dragging of InfiniteLines. Their visual position 
should be reflected in the TracedVariables self.hpos and self.vpos.

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.Crosshair.on_dragged_v
documentation:
Callback for dragging of InfiniteLines. Their visual position 
should be reflected in the TracedVariables self.hpos and self.vpos.

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.Crosshair.set_bounds
documentation: Set the area in which the infinitelines can be dragged.
Crosshair made up of two InfiniteLines.

classes.name: data_slicer.imageplot.ImagePlot

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.ImagePlot.__init__
documentation:
Allows setting of the image upon initialization. 

**Parameters**

==========  ============================================================
image       np.ndarray or pyqtgraph.ImageItem instance; the image to be
            displayed.
parent      QtWidget instance; parent widget of this widget.
background  str; confer PyQt documentation
name        str; allows giving a name for debug purposes
==========  ============================================================

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.ImagePlot.show_cursor
documentation:
Toggle whether or not to show a crosshair cursor that tracks the 
mouse movement.

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.ImagePlot.toggle_cursor
documentation: Change the visibility of the crosshair cursor.

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.ImagePlot.on_mouse_move
documentation:
Slot for mouse movement over the plot. Calculate the mouse
position in data coordinates and move the crosshair_cursor there.

**Parameters**

```




pos

QPointF object; x and y position of the mouse as returned by
.
```

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.ImagePlot.mousePressEvent
documentation:
Figure out where the click happened in data coordinates and make
the position available through the signal {signal}`sig_clicked
<data_slicer.imageplot.ImagePlot.sig_clicked>`.

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.ImagePlot.remove_image
documentation:
Removes the current image using the parent's {meth}`removeItem
pyqtgraph.PlotWidget.removeItem` function.

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.ImagePlot.set_image
documentation:
Expects either np.arrays or pg.ImageItems as input and sets them
correctly to this PlotWidget's Image with `addItem`. Also makes sure
there is only one Image by deleting the previous image.

Emits {signal}`sig_image_changed`

**Parameters**

```




image

np.ndarray or pyqtgraph.ImageItem instance; the image to be
displayed.

emit

bool; whether or not to emit 

(kw)args

positional and keyword arguments that are passed on to
```

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.ImagePlot.set_xscale
documentation:
Set the xscale of the plot. *xscale* is an array of the length
`len(self.image_item.shape[0])`.

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.ImagePlot.set_yscale
documentation:
Set the yscale of the plot. *yscale* is an array of the length
`len(self.image_item.image.shape[1])`.

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.ImagePlot._set_xscale
documentation:
Set the scale of the horizontal axis of the plot. *force* can be 
used to bypass the length checking.

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.ImagePlot._set_yscale
documentation:
Set the scale of the vertical axis of the plot. *force* can be 
used to bypass the length checking.

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.ImagePlot.transpose
documentation: Transpose the image, i.e. swap the x- and y-axes.

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.ImagePlot.set_xlabel
documentation: Shorthand for setting this plot's x axis label.

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.ImagePlot.set_ylabel
documentation: Shorthand for setting this plot's y axis label.

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.ImagePlot._set_axes_scales
documentation:
Transform the image such that it matches the desired x and y 
scales.

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.ImagePlot.get_limits
documentation: Return `[[x_min, x_max], [y_min, y_max]]`.

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.ImagePlot.fix_viewrange
documentation: Prevent zooming out by fixing the limits of the ViewBox.

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.ImagePlot.release_viewrange
documentation:
Undo the effects of {meth}`fix_viewrange
<data_slicer.imageplot.ImagePlot.fix_viewrange>`

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.ImagePlot._update_transform_factors
documentation:
Create a copy of the parameters that are necessary to reproduce
the current transform. This is necessary e.g. for the calculation of
the transform in {meth}`rotate
<data_slicer.imageplot.ImagePlot.rotate>`.

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.ImagePlot.rotate
documentation: Rotate the image_item by the given angle *alpha* (in degrees).

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.ImagePlot.mpl_export
documentation:
Export the content of this plot to a png image using matplotlib. 
The resulting image will have a white background and black ticklabes 
and should therefore be more readable than pyqtgraph's native plot
export options.

**Parameters**

=======  ===============================================================
figsize  tuple of float; (height, width) of figure in inches
title    str; figure title
xlabel   str; x axis label
ylabel   str; y axis label
dpi      int; png resolution in pixels per inch
args     positional arguments are absorbed and discarded (necessary 
         to connect this method to signal handling)
=======  ===============================================================

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.ImagePlot.lineplot
documentation:
Create a matplotlib plot with *n* lines extracted out of one of the
visible plots. The lines are normalized to their global maximum and
shifted from each other by *offset*.
See {func}`get_lines <data_slicer.utilities.get_lines>` for more
options on the extraction of the lines.

**Parameters**

```




plot

str; either "main" or "cut", specifies from which
plot to extract the lines.

dim

int; either 0 or 1, specifies in which direction to
take the lines.

ax

matplotlib.axes.Axes; the axes in which to plot.

n

int; number of lines to extract.

offset

float; spacing between neighboring lines.

lw

float; linewidth of the plotted lines.

color

any color argument understood by matplotlib; color
of the plotted lines.

label_fmt

str; a format string for the ticklabels.

n_ticks

int; number of ticks to print.

getlines_kwargs

other kwargs are passed to 
```

**Returns**

```




lines2ds

list of Line2D objects; the drawn lines.

xticks

list of float; locations of the 0 intensity value of
each line.

xtickvalues

list of float; if momenta were supplied, corresponding
xtick values in units of momenta. Otherwise this is
just a copy of xticks.

xticklabels

list of str; xtickvalues formatted according to
label_fmt.
```

A PlotWidget which mostly contains a single 2D image (intensity 
distribution) or a 3D array (distribution of RGB values) as well as all 
the nice pyqtgraph axes panning/rescaling/zooming functionality.

In addition, this allows one to use custom axes scales as opposed to 
being limited to pixel coordinates.

**Signals**

=================  =========================================================
sig_image_changed  emitted whenever the image is updated
sig_axes_changed   emitted when the axes are updated
sig_clicked        emitted when user clicks inside the imageplot
=================  =========================================================

classes.name: data_slicer.imageplot.CrosshairImagePlot

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.CrosshairImagePlot.update_allowed_values
documentation:
Update the allowed values silently. 
This assumes that the displayed image is in pixel coordinates and 
sets the allowed values to the available pixels.

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.CrosshairImagePlot.set_bounds
documentation:
Set both, the displayed area of the axis as well as the the range 
in which the crosshair can be dragged to the intervals [xmin, xmax] 
and [ymin, ymax].
An imageplot with a draggable crosshair.

classes.name: data_slicer.imageplot.CursorPlot

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.CursorPlot.__init__
documentation:
Initialize the slider and set up the visual tweaks to make a 
PlotWidget look more like a scalebar.

**Parameters**

===========  ============================================================
parent       QtWidget instance; parent widget of this widget
background   str; confer PyQt documentation
name         str; allows giving a name for debug purposes
orientation  str, `horizontal` or `vertical`; orientation of the cursor
===========  ============================================================

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.CursorPlot.get_data
documentation:
Get the currently displayed data as a tuple of arrays, one 
containing the x values and the other the y values.

**Returns**

=  =====================================================================
x  array containing the x values.
y  array containing the y values.
=  =====================================================================

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.CursorPlot.orientate
documentation: Define all aspects that are dependent on the orientation.

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.CursorPlot.register_traced_variable
documentation:
Set self.pos to the given TracedVariable instance and connect the 
relevant slots to the signals. This can be used to share a 
TracedVariable among widgets.

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.CursorPlot.on_position_change
documentation:
Callback for the {signal}`sigDragged
<pyqtgraph.InfiniteLine.sigDragged>`. Set the value of the
TracedVariable instance self.pos to the current slider position.

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.CursorPlot.on_allowed_values_change
documentation:
Callback for the {signal}`sig_allowed_values_changed
<data_slicer.utilities.TracedVariable.sig_allowed_values_changed>`.
With a change of the allowed values in the TracedVariable, we should
update our bounds accordingly.
The number of allowed values can also give us a hint for a reasonable
maximal width for the slider.

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.CursorPlot.set_position
documentation:
Callback for the {signal}`sig_value_changed
<data_slicer.utilities.TracedVariable.sig_value_changed>`. Whenever the
value of this TracedVariable is updated (possibly from outside this
Scalebar object), put the slider to the appropriate position.

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.CursorPlot.set_bounds
documentation:
Set both, the displayed area of the axis as well as the the range 
in which the slider (InfiniteLine) can be dragged to the interval 
[lower, upper].

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.CursorPlot.set_secondary_axis
documentation:
Create (or replace) a second x-axis on the top which ranges from 
`min_val` to `max_val`.
This is the right axis in case of the horizontal orientation.

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.CursorPlot.set_slider_pen
documentation:
Define the color and thickness of the slider (InfiniteLine
object {class}`pyqtgraph.InfiniteLine`) and store these attribute
in `self.slider_width` and `self.cursor_color`).

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.CursorPlot.increase_width
documentation:
Increase (or decrease) `self.slider_width` by `step` units of odd 
numbers (such that the line always has a well defined center at the 
value it is positioned at).

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.CursorPlot.increase_pos
documentation:
Increase (or decrease) `self.pos` by a reasonable amount. 
I.e. move `step` steps along the list of allowed values.

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.CursorPlot.keyPressEvent
documentation: Define responses to keyboard interactions.

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.CursorPlot.wheelEvent
documentation:
Override of the Qt wheelEvent method. Fired on mousewheel 
scrolling inside the widget.

Implements a simple, draggable scalebar represented by a line
({class}`pyqtgraph.InfiniteLine) on an axis
(:class:`pyqtgraph.PlotWidget).
The current position of the slider is tracked with the
\:class:`TracedVariable <data_slicer.utilities.TracedVariable>` self.pos
and its width with the `TracedVariable` self.slider_width.

classes.name: data_slicer.imageplot.Scalebar

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.Scalebar.set_size
documentation:
Set this widgets size by setting minimum and maximum sizes 
simultaneously to the same value.

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.Scalebar.keyPressEvent
documentation: Override some behaviour of the superclass.

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.Scalebar.add_text
documentation:
Add text to the scalebar.

**Parameters**

======  ================================================================
text    string; the text to be displayed.
pos     tuple; (x, y) position of the text relative to the scalebar.
anchor  tuple; (x, y) position of the text object's anchor.
======  ================================================================

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.Scalebar.set_relative_position
documentation:
Figure out this Scalebar's current size (in data units) and 
reposition its textItems accordingly.

methods.name: data_slicer.imageplot.Scalebar.on_allowed_values_changed
documentation: Keep TextItems in correct relative position.

Simple subclass of {class}`CursorPlot
<data_slicer.imageplot.CursorPlot>` that is intended to simulate a
scalebar. This is achieved by providing simply a long, flat plot without
any data and no y axis, but the same draggable slider as in CursorPlot.

**Attributes**

```




textItems

list of (t, (rx, ry)) tuples; t is a
 instance and rx, ry are floats in
the range [0, 1] indicating the relative positioning of the
textitems inside the Scalebar.
```
